Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I tried to set aside specific times to study, but with nine online courses happening simultaneously, I was constantly putting out fires. I'd tell myself I'd study in the evenings after work, but then I'd be too exhausted to focus. Most of my studying happened in desperate bursts when I realized something was due soon. I wanted to be more organized, but I just couldn't keep up with everything.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was most urgent. Some weeks I wouldn't touch certain courses at all because another one had a deadline looming. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd try to listen to lectures during my commute or while doing chores, but that meant my study times were scattered throughout the day. Weekends were supposed to be my catch-up time, but I'd often spend them recovering from the stress of the week instead.
Q3: Usually panic. I'd log in because I suddenly remembered an assignment was due, or I'd get an email notification that made my anxiety spike. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important, which I often had. It wasn't a calm, planned thing—it was more like damage control. The only times I logged in without stress were when I was genuinely curious about a lecture topic.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. I never got into a comfortable rhythm with the LMS because I was juggling so many courses. There were brief periods where I'd check it daily for maybe a week, but then life would get overwhelming and I'd fall off. The LMS felt more like a source of stress than a helpful tool. Every time I logged in, I'd see all the things I hadn't done yet.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful for me because I learn best by listening. I could play them while doing other things and still absorb information. The discussion forums were also helpful when other students explained concepts in different ways—sometimes hearing multiple perspectives helped things click. I appreciated when instructors posted audio summaries or podcasts because that matched how my brain works.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. I know they were probably valuable, but reading on a screen when I'm already exhausted just didn't work for me. I'd download them with good intentions but never actually get through them. The optional quizzes I mostly skipped too—if it wasn't graded, I couldn't justify spending limited energy on it when I was already behind on required work.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assessment or if I could see immediate practical value. With my limited energy and high anxiety, I had to triage everything. If something was going to help me pass or understand a core concept I was struggling with, I'd engage with it. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade got pushed aside because I simply didn't have the bandwidth.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones came with deadlines and consequences, so they demanded my attention even when I was exhausted. Optional ones, even if interesting, always got sacrificed when I was overwhelmed—which was most of the time. There's a guilt that comes with skipping optional stuff because you know it could help, but survival mode doesn't leave room for extras.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone during commutes and while cooking. I'd take voice memos of myself summarizing concepts and play them back. I also used YouTube videos and podcasts on related topics because sometimes external sources explained things better. A lot of my actual learning happened away from my computer entirely.
Q10: There were many times I was studying intensively but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three or four times to really understand it, but the system only saw one download. I'd have study sessions with a friend where we'd discuss course material for hours using our notes. I also spent a lot of time just thinking through problems while walking or lying in bed trying to sleep.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download videos or audio files once and then replay them multiple times offline. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so offline studying was often more practical anyway. I'd access a PDF once to download it, then read it—or try to read it—on my tablet later. The LMS definitely didn't capture how many times I actually engaged with materials I'd downloaded.
Q12: There were several points where I just hit a wall. Around midterms, my anxiety got so bad I could barely focus on anything. I'd stare at the screen and nothing would sink in. Work got really demanding for a few weeks, and I had to choose between sleeping and studying—I chose sleeping because I was falling apart. The CBT sessions helped somewhat, but the overwhelm kept coming back.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. Nine courses is too many—I know that now. The workload was crushing, and I was always confused about priorities. Fatigue made it hard to concentrate even when I did log in; I'd watch videos but zone out completely. My job and family responsibilities meant I couldn't give the courses the attention they deserved. The LMS became something I dreaded opening.
Q14: Many times. I'd log in and click through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but my brain wasn't absorbing anything. I'd have a lecture playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about deadlines or life stuff. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph five times without understanding it. Being physically present on the platform didn't mean I was mentally engaged at all.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even though the anxiety of knowing they were hanging over me was terrible. I'd avoid things that felt too difficult or confusing because starting them made me feel stupid and overwhelmed. The bigger the task seemed, the more I'd procrastinate. It's a vicious cycle—avoiding something makes it scarier, which makes you avoid it more. I knew I was sabotaging myself but couldn't stop.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd ignore something for weeks and then cram desperately in the final hours. The LMS would show these spikes of activity right before due dates, which is embarrassing but accurate. Without a deadline, I had no external pressure to overcome my inertia and anxiety. The problem is that deadline-driven work is lower quality and more stressful.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it sometimes motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But often feedback came too late to be useful, or I was too overwhelmed to process it properly. Negative feedback made my anxiety worse and sometimes made me avoid that course entirely for a while. Positive feedback was rare but when it happened, it gave me a small boost of hope that maybe I wasn't completely failing.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming. Having so many modules and deadlines across nine courses meant I could never feel on top of things. Courses with clearer, simpler structures were easier to engage with. When everything was spread across different tabs and sections in the LMS, I'd get lost and frustrated. I needed more streamlined, predictable structures to manage my cognitive load.
Q19: Courses with recorded audio lectures were much easier for me to engage with given my learning style. Courses that had everything due at once made engagement nearly impossible—I'd have to choose which ones to neglect. Clear rubrics helped because I didn't have to guess what was expected. Courses with lots of reading requirements were harder because that's not how I learn best, and I didn't have time to adapt.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well actually. You can be clicking around, watching videos, reading forums, genuinely trying to learn—but then the actual assignment feels too daunting to start. Or you run out of time because you spent too long trying to understand the material. High activity doesn't mean you've figured out how to translate that into completed work. Sometimes the activity is just anxious browsing.
Q21: That also makes sense to me. If someone learns efficiently offline or already has background knowledge, they might not need to spend hours on the LMS. I performed okay on exams because I'm good at listening and retaining information from lectures, even if I only watched them once on the platform. The LMS activity doesn't show what's happening in someone's head or what they're doing with downloaded materials.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the discussions with classmates outside the platform. They also can't distinguish between someone actively engaged and someone anxiously clicking around in a panic. The numbers don't capture the human experience of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when your brain is actively working on understanding something, asking questions, making connections. For me, engagement happens when I'm genuinely curious or when I can see how something applies to real life. It's not about time spent logged in—it's about mental investment and presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was actually trying despite my low completion rates. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures on my phone or the anxiety attacks I had before logging in. It wouldn't capture that I was genuinely interested in learning but drowning in too many responsibilities. My numbers look like someone who doesn't care, but I cared so much it was making me sick.
